title: How retiree discretionary spending is supporting consumer-facing CRE in the UK
gist: UK retiree discretionary spending grew 1.5% per annum in real terms from 2022 to 2024 while working-age spending declined, concentrating in recreation, culture, and hospitality; with retirement-age households projected to rise from 13 million to nearly 17 million by 2040 and a baby-boomer-to-Gen-X wealth transfer underway, retirees are becoming a structural driver of consumer-facing commercial real estate.

insights.claim: UK retiree discretionary spending grew 1.5% per annum in real terms from 2022 to 2024, contrasting sharply with declines in working-age discretionary spending over the same period.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Identifies retirees as the resilient consumer cohort in a weak UK environment, favoring retailers and CRE landlords exposed to older demographics.
insights.quote:
a growth rate of
1.5% per annum in real terms from 2022 to 2024, contrasting sharply with
declines in working-age discretionary spending

insights.claim: Growth in UK retiree spending is concentrated in recreation, culture, and hospitality, with restaurants and hotels increasing at 5.4% annually.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Points to leisure, dining, and experiential CRE as the segments capturing retiree wallet share, useful for targeting consumer-facing real estate investment.
insights.quote:
notable increases in restaurants and hotels at
5.4% annually

insights.claim: The UK population of retirement-age households is projected to rise from 13 million to nearly 17 million by 2040, expanding demand for leisure and experiential services.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: A multi-decade demographic tailwind underpins long-term demand for consumer-facing CRE oriented to retirees.
insights.quote:
projected to
rise from 13 million to nearly 17 million by 2040

insights.claim: A significant wealth transfer from baby boomers to Generation X is underway in the UK, which is expected to further support discretionary spending patterns.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Intergenerational wealth transfer extends the retiree-spending tailwind to the next cohort, reinforcing durable demand for consumer CRE.
insights.quote:
a significant wealth transfer from baby boomers to Generation X is
underway

insights.claim: Despite a challenging current economic environment in the UK, the long-term trajectory for retiree spending remains positive, driven by stable income sources and an increasing number of households.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Frames retiree-linked consumer CRE as a defensive, structurally growing exposure even amid near-term macro weakness.
insights.quote:
the long-term trajectory for
retiree spending remains positive, driven by stable income sources and an
increasing number of households
